symptoms: dark brown water-soaked patches on leaves
white mould on leaf undersides
stems turning black
fruit develops dark firm rot
rapid plant collapse in humid weather
treatment_en: Spray mancozeb 75% WP at 2g/litre or cymoxanil+mancozeb at label rate
Remove and destroy all infected plant parts immediately
Avoid overhead watering; water at base only
Apply copper oxychloride as a protective spray during humid spells
prevention: Use resistant varieties; maintain wide plant spacing; avoid waterlogged soil; do not plant in same field two seasons running
season: Kharif and Rabi, especially high humidity and cool nights
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu

symptoms: dark brown spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern)
yellowing around lesions
spots appear first on older lower leaves
stem cankers near soil
fruit lesions at stem end
treatment_en: Apply mancozeb or chlorothalonil fungicide at 10-day intervals
Remove infected lower leaves to reduce spore load
Ensure adequate potassium fertilisation to strengthen plants
Mulch around base to reduce soil splash
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed; practice 2-3 year crop rotation; remove crop debris after harvest
season: Any season; worse in warm humid conditions
region: All tomato-growing states of India

symptoms: leaves curl upward and inward
leaves turn yellow and leathery
stunted plant growth
flowers drop without setting fruit
small pale whiteflies visible under leaves
treatment_en: Control whitefly with imidacloprid 17.8 SL at 0.3 ml/litre or thiamethoxam 25 WG
Remove and burn heavily infected plants
Apply reflective mulch to repel whiteflies
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce whitefly population
prevention: Use virus-resistant or tolerant varieties (eg. Arka Rakshak); install 40-mesh insect-proof net for nursery; remove weed hosts nearby
season: Year-round; worse in hot dry conditions
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu

symptoms: yellowing and wilting of leaves starting on one side of plant
brown discolouration inside stem when cut
plants wilt during the day and recover slightly at night
eventual plant death
treatment_en: No curative chemical treatment; remove and destroy infected plants
Drench soil with carbendazim 50 WP at 1g/litre around healthy plants as protection
Maintain soil pH between 6.5-7 to reduce fungal activity
prevention: Use resistant varieties; practice 4-year crop rotation; solarise soil before planting; avoid waterlogging
season: Warm weather above 25°C
region: All tomato-growing regions; widespread

symptoms: circular holes in fruit
frass (excrement) visible at entry holes
caterpillar found inside fruit
premature fruit drop
damaged fruit quickly rots
treatment_en: Spray spinosad 45 SC at 0.3 ml/litre or emamectin benzoate 5 SG at 0.4g/litre
Install pheromone traps for Helicoverpa at 5 traps per acre
Collect and destroy damaged fruits daily
Apply NPV (nuclear polyhedrosis virus) spray at 250 LE/hectare
prevention: Inter-crop with marigold as a trap crop; encourage natural predators; avoid excessive nitrogen which attracts pests
season: Year-round; peaks in Kharif
region: All tomato-growing states

symptoms: circular holes in bolls
damaged bolls fail to open
caterpillars visible inside bolls
early stage larvae damage flower buds
frass at entry points
treatment_en: Spray emamectin benzoate 5 SG at 0.4g/litre or chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC at 0.3 ml/litre
Install Helicoverpa pheromone traps at 5 per acre
Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays in early infestation
Remove and destroy damaged bolls
prevention: Use Bt cotton varieties where approved; practice crop rotation with non-host crops; conserve natural enemies
season: Kharif (July–November)
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Punjab

symptoms: leaves curl downward
thickening and darkening of leaf veins
small cup-shaped leafy outgrowths (enations) on leaf underside
stunted growth
whitefly colonies visible under leaves
treatment_en: Apply thiamethoxam 25 WG at 0.3g/litre or flonicamid 50 WG to control whitefly vector
Remove heavily infected plants
Spray neem-based products (azadirachtin) to reduce whitefly
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation
prevention: Use virus-tolerant varieties; plant trap crops of okra; maintain weed-free fields; install yellow sticky traps
season: Kharif
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan

symptoms: white cottony mass on stems, leaves, and bolls
yellowing and wilting of leaves
sticky honeydew secretion on plant surface
black sooty mould developing on honeydew
plants look dusty white
treatment_en: Spray profenofos 50 EC at 2 ml/litre or chlorpyrifos 20 EC at 2.5 ml/litre
Release Cryptolaemus montrouzieri (mealybug destroyer) biological control at 10 per plant
Remove heavily infested plant parts
Spray with water jet first to dislodge colonies
prevention: Regular scouting from germination; control ants which protect mealybugs; destroy volunteer cotton plants
season: Year-round; peaks in dry conditions
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Gujarat, Maharashtra

symptoms: diamond-shaped spots with grey centre and brown border on leaves
neck of panicle turns brown and rots (neck blast)
grain filling poor in affected panicles
spots on collar, nodes, and grains
plants lodge when node blast occurs
treatment_en: Apply tricyclazole 75 WP at 0.6g/litre or isoprothiolane 40 EC at 1.5 ml/litre at first sign
Repeat spray after 10 days if disease pressure high
Drain fields and allow to dry briefly to slow spread
Reduce nitrogen application to avoid lush growth
prevention: Use blast-resistant varieties (eg. MTU 1010); avoid excess nitrogen; maintain proper water level; use certified seed
season: Kharif; worst in cool humid nights with warm days
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Odisha, West Bengal, Assam

symptoms: wavy yellow to white lesions starting from leaf tips and margins
lesions spread down the leaf
leaves dry out and die
milky or yellow bacterial ooze on lesion surface in early morning
kresek (wilting at tillering stage) in severe cases
treatment_en: Drain fields immediately and stop nitrogen application
Spray copper oxychloride 50 WP at 3g/litre as protective measure
No highly effective curative chemical exists; manage agronomically
Remove infected tillers from field borders
prevention: Use resistant varieties (eg. IR64, Swarna Sub1); avoid high nitrogen doses; use disease-free seed; avoid waterlogging
season: Kharif; peaks after floods or heavy rain
region: All paddy-growing states; major problem in AP, Telangana, Tamil Nadu

cause: Bipolaris oryzae (fungus); worsened by nutrient deficiency
symptoms: small to large brown oval spots on leaves
spots have grey or white centre with brown margin
similar spots on leaf sheath and glumes
grain discolouration
plants turn brown and look scorched
treatment_en: Spray mancozeb 75 WP at 2g/litre or propiconazole 25 EC at 1 ml/litre
Apply potassium and phosphorus fertilisers to correct deficiency
Treat seed with thiram 75 WP at 2g/kg before sowing
prevention: Correct soil nutrient deficiencies; use balanced fertilisation; treat seeds; plant resistant varieties
season: Kharif; associated with nutrient-poor soils
region: All paddy-growing states

symptoms: dead heart: central tiller dries and dies at vegetative stage
white ear: panicle is empty and white at reproductive stage
transverse holes visible in stem
caterpillar inside stem when cut open
frass in stem
treatment_en: Apply cartap hydrochloride 4G granules at 18 kg/ha into standing water
Spray chlorpyrifos 20 EC at 2 ml/litre or chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC
Install light traps to attract and kill adult moths
Clip tips of seedlings before transplanting to remove eggs
prevention: Avoid close spacing; remove stubble after harvest; maintain water level; use resistant varieties
season: Kharif; peaks at tillering and panicle initiation
region: All paddy states; especially AP, Telangana, Tamil Nadu

symptoms: yellow-orange powdery pustules in stripes along leaf veins
pustules arranged in rows
yellowing and drying of leaves
rapid spread in cool humid weather
severe cases: entire field turns yellow
treatment_en: Spray propiconazole 25 EC at 1 ml/litre or tebuconazole 250 EC at 1 ml/litre
Apply at first sign; repeat after 15 days if needed
Avoid excessive nitrogen; maintain balanced fertilisation
prevention: Use resistant varieties (eg. HD 2967, WH 1105); plant on time (avoid late sowing); monitor regularly
season: Rabi; cool temperatures 10-15°C; peak February-March
region: Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh

symptoms: white powdery fungal growth on upper leaf surface
white colonies also on sheaths and glumes
powdery patches turn grey-brown with age
affected leaves yellow and die
pustules visible as black dots within white mass
treatment_en: Spray propiconazole 25 EC at 1 ml/litre or hexaconazole 5 EC at 1.5 ml/litre
Apply sulphur 80 WP at 2g/litre as an alternative
prevention: Avoid dense planting; use resistant varieties; reduce nitrogen input
season: Rabi; cool moist conditions in February-March
region: Northern plains, hills; Punjab, Haryana, HP

symptoms: circular sunken lesions on ripe and green fruits
lesions have dark border and salmon-pink spore masses at centre
tip of branches die back from top downward
infected fruits shrivel
seeds inside may be infected
treatment_en: Spray mancozeb 75 WP at 2g/litre or carbendazim 50 WP at 1g/litre
Alternate with copper oxychloride sprays
Remove and destroy infected fruits and branches
Treat seed with thiram or carbendazim before sowing
prevention: Use disease-free certified seed; avoid overhead irrigation; practice crop rotation; remove crop debris
season: Any season; worse in warm humid weather
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu

cause: Thrips tabaci / Scirtothrips dorsalis feeding; also Chilli Leaf Curl Virus
symptoms: leaves curl upward and inward
leaf surface becomes rough and crinkled
silvery streaks on leaves
flower buds drop
stunted plant with distorted shoots
tiny yellowish insects visible
treatment_en: Spray spinosad 45 SC at 0.3 ml/litre or fipronil 5 SC at 1.5 ml/litre for thrips
Use imidacloprid 17.8 SL at 0.3 ml/litre as systemic insecticide
Install blue sticky traps to monitor thrips (25 per acre)
Remove and destroy severely infected plants
prevention: Use resistant or tolerant varieties; avoid planting near onion fields; plant border crops of maize or sorghum
season: Year-round; worse in hot dry conditions
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka

symptoms: white powdery patches on upper and lower leaf surfaces
yellowing of leaves below powdery growth
leaves curl and drop prematurely
severe defoliation of lower canopy
treatment_en: Spray wettable sulphur 80 WP at 3g/litre or hexaconazole 5 EC at 1 ml/litre
Repeat every 10-15 days during disease pressure
prevention: Maintain proper spacing; avoid excess nitrogen; do not irrigate in the evening
season: Hot dry weather; March-May
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Rajasthan

cause: Cercospora arachidicola (early) and Cercosporidium personatum (late) — fungi
symptoms: early leaf spot: small brown circular spots with yellow halo on upper leaf surface
late leaf spot: dark brown to black spots without yellow halo
severe defoliation from bottom upward
petiole and stem lesions in heavy infection
treatment_en: Spray mancozeb 75 WP at 2g/litre at 35 days after sowing and repeat every 10-15 days
Apply chlorothalonil 75 WP at 2g/litre as an alternative
3-4 sprays usually required through the season
prevention: Use tolerant varieties (eg. ICGV 86031); practice crop rotation; remove infected crop debris; maintain proper spacing
season: Kharif; peaks in humid conditions after 35 days of crop growth
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Rajasthan

symptoms: rotting and darkening at collar region (junction of stem and root)
plant wilts rapidly and dies
black fungal growth visible at collar
seedling death in early stages
roots rot and turn brown
treatment_en: Treat seed with Trichoderma viride at 4g/kg before sowing
Apply carbendazim + thiram (1:1) at 3g/kg as seed treatment
Drench soil around affected plants with copper oxychloride 3g/litre
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
prevention: Use disease-free seed; avoid waterlogging; treat seeds; maintain soil health with organic matter
season: Kharif; common in early crop growth phase
region: Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Rajasthan

symptoms: ragged holes in leaves
frass (sawdust-like excrement) in the funnel/whorl of young plants
caterpillar feeds in the whorl
head capsule visible in frass
later attacks cob and causes direct grain damage
young plants may be completely destroyed
treatment_en: Apply emamectin benzoate 5 SG at 0.4g/litre into the whorl at first sign
Spray spinetoram 11.7 SC at 0.5 ml/litre or chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC at 0.4 ml/litre
Apply Bt-based biopesticide into the whorl for early infestation
Use pheromone traps to monitor adult moth activity
prevention: Early sowing; monitor from emergence; conserve natural enemies; intercrop with cowpea
season: Kharif; arrives with monsoon, peaks July-September
region: All maize-growing states; major threat since 2018

symptoms: white downy growth on lower leaf surface
leaves turn pale yellow and striped
affected plants are stunted and fail to produce cob
excessive tillering (bushy appearance)
entire plant may turn chlorotic white
treatment_en: Treat seed with metalaxyl 35 WS at 6g/kg before sowing (most effective prevention)
Spray metalaxyl + mancozeb (Ridomil MZ) at 2.5g/litre at first sign
Remove and destroy infected plants from field
prevention: Use resistant hybrids; treat seed with metalaxyl; avoid planting near sorghum; plant early
season: Kharif; favoured by cool wet weather early in season
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu

symptoms: small white sunken lesions on leaves initially
lesions enlarge with purple centre and yellow margin
affected leaves bend and collapse
disease spreads to neck of bulb
bulbs develop soft rot in storage
treatment_en: Spray mancozeb 75 WP at 2g/litre or iprodione 50 WP at 1g/litre at first sign
Repeat at 7-10 day intervals
Apply copper oxychloride as an alternate spray
Avoid dense planting
prevention: Use disease-free sets; crop rotation; avoid overhead irrigation; apply nitrogen in splits
season: Any season; worse during warm humid periods
region: Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Gujarat

symptoms: silvery white streaks on leaves
leaves turn pale and tip burn
leaves have scratched or blotchy appearance
plants are stunted
tiny yellow insects visible on leaves and inside layers
heavy infestation causes leaf drying
treatment_en: Spray spinosad 45 SC at 0.3 ml/litre or fipronil 5 SC at 1.5 ml/litre
Use imidacloprid 17.8 SL at 0.3 ml/litre as systemic option
Install blue sticky traps at 25 per acre
Add spreader-sticker to spray solution for better coverage on waxy leaves
prevention: Use blue sticky traps for early detection; avoid water stress; plant resistant varieties where available
season: Year-round; worst in dry conditions
region: All onion-growing states

cause: Mungbean Yellow Mosaic Virus (MYMV) transmitted by whitefly
symptoms: bright yellow mosaic patches on leaves
leaves show irregular yellow and green patches
infected plants are severely stunted
pods are fewer and smaller
whitefly visible on leaf undersides
treatment_en: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately to prevent spread
Control whitefly with thiamethoxam 25 WG at 0.3g/litre or imidacloprid at 0.3 ml/litre
Do not save seed from infected plants
prevention: Use resistant varieties (eg. JS 335 where available); plant early to avoid peak whitefly season; maintain weed-free fields
season: Kharif
region: Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Rajasthan

symptoms: internal red discolouration of cane stalk with white patches (cross-sectional view)
external wilting and drying of leaves
cane becomes hollow with sour vinegar smell
leaves turn yellow and dry out from top downward
withered appearance of ratoon crop
treatment_en: Use disease-free setts for planting; treat setts with carbendazim 50 WP at 1g/litre for 30 minutes before planting
Rogue out and destroy infected clumps
Avoid injuries during intercultural operations
Do not ratoon heavily infected fields
prevention: Use resistant varieties; treat seed setts; practice 2-year crop rotation with non-host crops; avoid waterlogging
season: Year-round; peaks during waterlogged conditions
region: Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, UP

symptoms: pale yellowish insects visible on undersides of leaves
honeydew secreted by pyrilla causes sooty mould
leaves covered in black sooty mould
plant growth is reduced
juice extraction and sugar recovery reduced
treatment_en: Apply malathion 50 EC at 1.5 ml/litre or dimethoate 30 EC at 1.5 ml/litre
Release Epiricania melanoleuca egg parasitoid as biological control (very effective)
Remove and burn dried leaves to reduce habitat
prevention: Conserve parasitoids; avoid excessive nitrogen; clean irrigation channels; early detection through monitoring
season: Year-round; peaks in July-October
region: UP, Punjab, Haryana, Andhra Pradesh

symptoms: clusters of small soft greenish-yellow insects on undersides of young leaves
leaves curl downward and become cupped
sticky honeydew on leaf surface
black sooty mould on honeydew
premature leaf drop
plant growth slows
treatment_en: Spray dimethoate 30 EC at 1.5 ml/litre or imidacloprid 17.8 SL at 0.3 ml/litre
Apply neem oil 3% spray as organic option
Encourage natural predators like ladybird beetles and lacewings
Spray with high-pressure water jet to dislodge colonies
prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen; plant border crop of sorghum; conserve natural enemies; monitor early
season: Kharif; peaks in dry weather August-September
region: All cotton-growing states

symptoms: oval or elliptical greenish-grey lesions on leaf sheaths
lesions have irregular brown border
lesions coalesce and move up the plant
leaves above infected sheath die
white cottony fungal growth at the water line
small brown sclerotia visible near water surface
treatment_en: Spray propiconazole 25 EC at 1 ml/litre or hexaconazole 5 EC at 1.5 ml/litre at tillering
Apply validamycin 3 L at 2 ml/litre for early control
Drain field for 3-4 days to reduce humidity at canopy
Avoid dense planting; reduce nitrogen dose
prevention: Maintain proper plant spacing; avoid excess nitrogen; remove ratoon; use tolerant varieties
season: Kharif; worst in warm humid conditions with dense planting
region: All paddy states; major problem in AP, Telangana, Tamil Nadu

symptoms: white raised spots with dark centres on fruit (bird's eye spot)
brown streaks inside stem when cut
wilting and marginal leaf scorch
yellowing of leaves starting from edges
stem splits and oozes bacterial slime
treatment_en: Remove and destroy infected plants; no cure once plant is infected
Spray copper-based bactericides (copper hydroxide 77 WP) as protectant on healthy plants
Disinfect tools with 10% bleach between plants
Do not save seed from infected crop
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed; avoid overhead irrigation; practice 3-year rotation; disinfect tools
season: Any season; worse in warm conditions
region: All tomato-growing states

symptoms: entire ear replaced by a mass of black powdery spores
black smutted ears appear at or just before heading
spores blow away quickly leaving bare rachis
infected plants otherwise look normal until heading
treatment_en: Treat seed with carboxin 37.5% + thiram 37.5% DS at 3g/kg — the only reliable control
Alternatively use tebuconazole 2% DS at 1.5g/kg
Remove smutted ears before spores are released
prevention: Always use certified treated seed; rogue smutted plants early; grow resistant varieties
season: Rabi; infection at flowering carried over in seed
region: Punjab, Haryana, UP, Rajasthan

symptoms: white cottony growth at base of stem
stem rots at ground level
plant wilts suddenly and dies
small tan-brown sclerotia (mustard seed sized) visible in white mycelium
roots and pods may also rot
treatment_en: Apply Trichoderma viride 4g/kg as seed treatment before sowing
Drench soil with carbendazim 50 WP at 2g/litre around affected plants
Remove infected plants with surrounding soil to limit spread
prevention: Treat seeds with bioagents; deep plough to bury sclerotia; avoid waterlogging; crop rotation with cereals
season: Kharif; worse in warm moist soils
region: Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Rajasthan

symptoms: long elliptical greyish-green to tan lesions on leaves
lesions 5-15 cm long with wavy margins
dark grey spores visible in lesions
severe infection causes large portions of leaf to die
lower leaves affected first, disease moves upward
treatment_en: Spray mancozeb 75 WP at 2g/litre or propiconazole 25 EC at 1 ml/litre at first sign
Repeat at 15-day intervals during wet weather
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids; practice crop rotation; remove crop residue after harvest
season: Kharif; favoured by moderate temperatures and heavy dew
region: All maize states; hill areas especially affected

symptoms: tan to dark brown angular lesions on leaf undersides
small raised pustules (uredinia) on lower leaf surface
upper leaf surface shows pale green to yellow spots
heavy infection causes rapid defoliation
affected leaves dry and drop prematurely
treatment_en: Spray propiconazole 25 EC at 1 ml/litre or tebuconazole 250 EC at 1 ml/litre at first sign
Repeat every 14-21 days during disease pressure
Ensure good spray coverage on leaf undersides
prevention: Plant early; monitor regularly from pod fill stage; use resistant varieties; avoid excessively dense canopy
season: Kharif; worst in warm humid conditions at pod fill
region: Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Rajasthan, Karnataka

symptoms: long black whip-like structure emerging from growing point of cane
whip covered in black powdery spores
infected plants are stunted
thin grass-like shoots produced instead of normal cane
ratoon crops more heavily affected
treatment_en: Use disease-free planting material; treat setts in hot water at 50°C for 2 hours before planting
Apply propiconazole 25 EC at 1 ml/litre to setts before planting
Rogue and destroy smutted clumps before spores disperse
prevention: Plant resistant varieties; treat setts with hot water; destroy affected clumps immediately
season: Year-round; spores spread by wind
region: Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, UP, Karnataka
